coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive detection of sulfasalazine based on the fluorescence quenching of a terbium complex probe
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Jiahui
Yu, Yongli
Kong, Baoying
Zhao, Shuang
coredata.dc:description: In the current study, a method for the determination of sulfasalazine (SSZ) based on the fluorescence quenching of Tb-2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (Tb-DPA) complex probe in the presence of sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) was established. Moreover, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was studied and it was concluded that the process is static quenching and involved the formation of the SSZ-Tb-DPA ternary complex. Under the optimum conditions, the linear range of the method was 8.0 × 10−8–1.00 × 10−6 M and 1.00 × 10−6–1.00 × 10−4 M with the detection limit of 60 nM. The approach was successfully applied for the detection of SSZ in pharmaceutical preparations, urine, and human serum samples with recoveries between 98% and 106%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Terbium complex
Sulfasalazine
Fluorescence probe
2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of SSZ in tablets and biological samples
Determination of the mechanism for the SSZ detection
Effect of pH and the buffer solution
Stability and reaction time of the probe
Detection of SSZ in tablets and biological samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China ( 51909031 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of the Tb-DPA complex in the presence of different SSZ concentrations (from 0 to j: 0, 0.080, 0.40, 0.80, 4.00, 8.00, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, and 80.0 μM) in a pH 7.6 Tris–HCl system.
The absorption spectra of 80 μM SSZ in the presence of Tb3+ at different concentrations (from 0 to f: 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80 μM). .

(a) Fluorescence intensity of the Tb-DPA probe at different pH values; (b) Fluorescence intensity of the Tb-DPA probe at different pH values adjusted with the Tris–HCl buffer solution; (c) The effect of the SDBS concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the probe (I0 : in the absence of SSZ, I: in the presence of SSZ).
Schematic diagram of the Tb-DPA complex for the detection of SSZ.
Comparison of analytical parameters of different methods for SSZ detection.
Tolerance limits of diverse substances with respect to 0.80 μM SSZ using the complex probe.
Determination of SSZ in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive detection of sulfasalazine based on the fluorescence quenching of a terbium complex probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, a method for the determination of sulfasalazine (SSZ) based on the fluorescence quenching of Tb-2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (Tb-DPA) complex probe in the presence of sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) was established. Moreover, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was studied and it was concluded that the process is static quenching and involved the formation of the SSZ-Tb-DPA ternary complex. Under the optimum conditions, the linear range of the method was 8.0 × 10−8–1.00 × 10−6 M and 1.00 × 10−6–1.00 × 10−4 M with the detection limit of 60 nM. The approach was successfully applied for the detection of SSZ in pharmaceutical preparations, urine, and human serum samples with recoveries between 98% and 106%.

ce:section.ce:para: SSZ is a synthetic pharmaceutical compound, which is primarily used for treating ulcerative colitis [1], rheumatoid arthritis [2, 3], and Crohn's disease [3]. However, excessive use of SSZ is known to cause numerous adverse reactions, including severe allergy, hemopoietin system disorder, and antibiotic resistance. Analysis of data obtained from biological samples, such as urine and blood, could provide important information for the determination of the human tolerance dose, absorption, toxicity, and metabolism. Therefore, the establishment of SSZ detection method is of great significance in clinical medicine.

A number of analytical methods have been explored for the detection of SSZ in the past, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–6], capillary electrophoresis mass spectrometry (CE-MS) [7], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [8], electrochemical detection [9–12], and fluorometry [13–16]. Among the aforementioned methods, the equipment used in HPLC and CE-MS is relatively expensive. Furthermore, Preparation of electrodes is time consuming in electrochemical analysis [9–11]. All of the mentioned limitations prevent the widespread application of these methods.

Because SSZ has no intrinsic fluorescence [13], it cannot be detected directly by spectrofluorimetry and only few reports on the determination of this compound fluorometrically currently exist. For example, Zhang et al. [15] fabricated nitrogen-doped carbon dots using citric acid to detect SSZ. In addition, Jiang et al. [16] synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots based on the ginkgo leaf for the detection of SSZ in mouse plasma. In the above analytical methods, the researchers first prepared nano-fluorescent probes, which were utilized to detect SSZ. The preparation of nanoprobes usually requires relatively harsh reaction conditions and long reaction times, which somewhat limits the application of these methods in routine sample analysis.

In comparison to nanoprobes, the preparation of complex fluorescent probes is simple and fast. Probes of this type have been applied in the detection of PO4 3−, benzimidazole drugs, periodate, and mebendazole [17–20]. Moreover, lanthanide complex fluorescent probes exhibit some unusual spectroscopic properties, including narrow emission band, a large Stokes shift, and a long fluorescence lifetime. Hydrogen sulfide, Hg2+, alkaline phosphatase, and 6-thioguanine were successfully detected using probes of this type [21–24]. Recently, a number of research studies involving application of terbium-doped nanoparticles to determine DPA based on the combination of Tb3+ and DPA have been reported in the literature concerning lanthanide fluorescence nanoprobes [25–28]; however, there is no report on the use of Tb3+ and DPA complex probe in analytical assays.

In this work, a method of detecting SSZ based on terbium complex fluorescent probe was developed. The probe was synthesized by the coordination of a terbium ion with DPA in the presence of SDBS, which emits characteristic fluorescence peak of Tb3+ due to the sensitization effect of DPA. When SSZ was added into the solution of the complex probe, the fluorescence quenching of the probe was observed due to the combination of SSZ with the Tb-DPA complex. In addition, the quenching extent was determined to be linearly correlated to the concentration of SSZ. Finally, the established method was successfully utilized to determine SSZ in the drug, urine, and serum samples. To the best of our knowledge, no fluorescence detection method for SSZ based on a lanthanide complex probe has been reported so far.

Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were recorded on a Carry Eclipse luminescence spectrometer (Varian, USA). In all fluorescence measurements, excitation and emission wavelengths were 272 and 546 nm, respectively. Absorption spectra were measured with a Lambda-365 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) using a 10 mm path length quartz cell.

The 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) was purchased from Shanghai Crystal Pure Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); SSZ was obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) was purchased from Sinopharm Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Tb4O7 (99.99%) was obtained from Beijing Youyan Rare Earth New Materials Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). A stock solution of 1.00 × 10−3 M SSZ was prepared by dissolving SSZ powder in a 0.1 M aqueous NaOH solution, which was subsequently transferred to a volumetric flask for constant volume using water. 1.00 × 10−2 M DPA was prepared in water. A stock solution of 2.00 × 10−2 M TbCl3 was obtained by dissolving Tb4O7 with a hydrochloric acid solution (V HCl:V H2O = 3:1), evaporating the solution to nearly dry on a hot plate, and then cooling to room temperature and diluting with water. All reagents were of analytical grade and water was ultrapure (18.25 MΩ cm).

At room temperature, 0.600 mL of DPA solution (1.00 × 10−4 M) was added to a series of volumetric flasks, respectively, followed by 0.800 mL of TbCl3 solution (1.00 × 10−3 M), 1.00 mL of Tris–HCl buffer solution (pH 7.6) and 1.00 mL of SDBS solution (1.00 × 10−3 M), and then these flasks were shaken for 20 min. Subsequently, different volumes of SSZ solution (2.00 × 10−4 M) were added into the above solutions, which were diluted up to 10.0 mL with water and shaken well. Lastly, a 272 nm excitation wavelength was used for the emission spectra and the excitation spectra were recorded with an emission wavelength of 546 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SSZ in tablets and biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: Ten sulfasalazine tablets (each containing 250 mg SSZ) were weighed accurately and then grinded thoroughly with a mortar. 0.0609 g of the SSZ powder was weighted and sonicated in 50 mL of water containing 0.2 mL of a 0.1 M NaOH solution for 10 min. The sample solution was filtered and transferred to a 100 mL calibrated flask for constant volume. The above solution was appropriately diluted for the determination of SSZ.

The urine and human serum samples were offered by the Hospital of Northeastern University. The urine sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane before being diluted five times with water. No further pretreatment was required. In Three serum samples, 0.400 mL each, were spiked with 0.800 mL of a SSZ solution at different concentrations. 2 mL of acetonitrile was subsequently added separately to deproteinize the serum. The precipitated proteins were separated by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatants were used for the determination of SSZ.

In the current study, experiments allowing for the evaluation of the reaction of DPA and Tb3+were carried out. Fig. S1.a and S1.b illustrates the excitation (λem = 546 nm) and emission (λex = 272 nm) spectra of the three investigated solutions: Tb3+ (1), Tb3+ and DPA (2), and DPA (3). As shown in Figure S1.a and S1.b, no excitation or emission peaks can be observed for Tb3+ or DPA. Furthermore, an excitation peak at 272 nm and emission peaks at 490, 546, 588, and 622 nm was seen for the solution of Tb3+ and DPA. Because the excitation peak at 272 nm was attributed to DPA and the emission peaks at 490, 546, 588, and 622 nm corresponded to Tb3+, it could be concluded that in the solution of Tb3+ and DPA, Tb3+reacted with DPA to produce a complex. In addition, there was an energy transfer in the complex, which involved absorption of energy of a wavelength of 272 nm by DPA, and then transfer of energy to Tb3+, resulting in the emission of characteristic peaks by Tb3+.

Furthermore, the Tb-DPA complex was tested as a fluorescent probe for detecting SSZ. Different concentrations of SSZ were added to the Tb-DPA solutions in the presence of SDBS, and the emission spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence of the Tb-DPA complex was quenched by SSZ, and the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase of the SSZ concentration. Based on these observations, the fluorescence method for the determination of SSZ was developed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the mechanism for the SSZ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method is based on the fluorescence quenching of the Tb-DPA probe by SSZ. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching can be divided into two types: stable quenching and dynamic quenching, and the method to distinguish between the two types is to measure the fluorescence lifetime of the probe before and after quenching [29–31]. If no change of the lifetime is observed, it is a static quenching process; otherwise, it is dynamic quenching.

The fluorescence lifetime of the Tb-DPA probe in the absence and presence of SSZ was tested. According to the regression equations shown in Figure S2, the fluorescence lifetimes of the Tb-DPA probe were 0.5557 ms and 0.5559 ms in the absence and presence of SSZ, respectively. Because these values were nearly the same, it could be concluded that the fluorescence quenching by SSZ belonged to the static type. That is, the fluorescence quenching of the probe was due to the formation of a new compound upon the addition of SSZ into the Tb-DPA solution.

To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports concerning the reactivity of SSZ and Tb3+. A series of SSZ solutions containing different concentrations of Tb3+ were prepared in the present study, and the absorption spectra were measured. As the concentration of Tb3+ increased, the maximum absorption wavelength of SSZ shifted from 360 nm to 378 nm and the absorbance decreased, which suggested that SSZ combined with Tb3+ to form a complex (Fig. 2 ). According to the molecular structure of SSZ, it can be deduced that Tb3+ combines with the oxygen atoms of the hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties, which are ortho position on the benzene ring, forming a six-membered ring [14].

Tb3+ is a 9 coordination ion and in the Tb-DPA complex DPA occupies just three coordination sites [32]. Therefore, upon addition of SSZ into the solution of Tb-DPA, Tb3+ can further combine with SSZ to produce a ternary complex. Because SSZ is a non-fluorescent molecule, the formation of SSZ-Tb-DPA led to the fluorescence quenching of the probe. The mechanism of the detection of SSZ using the Tb-DPA probe is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The formation constant for the reaction of SSZ with the Tb-DPA complex can be calculated based on the Stern-Volmer equation [18, 33]:
I 0
/ I = 1 + K
[ Q ]
where I0 and I are the emission intensities of the Tb-DPA probe in the absence and presence of SSZ, respectively, K is the formation constant, and [Q] denotes the concentration of SSZ.

As shown in Figure S3, the relationship between I0/I and [Q] is exceptionally linear, and the linear regression equation was given as I0/I = 1.006 + 2.7 × 105[Q], with the correlation coefficient of 0.9986, which indicated that the binding ratio of SSZ to Tb-DPA complex was 1:1. From the linear regression equation, the formation constant was 2.7 × 105 L/mol, which suggested that SSZ-Tb-DPA is a relatively stable compound.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and the buffer solution
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of the solution affects the presence and reactivity of the substances, including Tb3+, DPA, and SSZ; therefore, finding the optimal pH value is important. Solutions of the Tb-DPA probe in the absence and presence of SSZ at different pH values were prepared by adjusting with NaOH or HCl, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded. Minor changes in the fluorescence intensities of the probe without SSZ were observed in the pH range of 4.5~7.5; however, an evident decrease was noted as the pH value increased (Fig. 3 a). The formation of terbium hydroxide may be the explanation for the significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity at pH > 7.5. Upon addition of SSZ into the solution of the probe, the fluorescence quenching occurred at all pH values except for 8.5 and 9.0. Furthermore, the largest quenching occurred at the pH value of 7.5.

To keep the pH value of the solution stable, a buffer solution should be used. For the purposes of our study the Tris–HCl buffer was selected. Solutions of the probe in the absence and presence of SSZ at different pH values from 7.0 to 8.2 were prepared and adjusted with Tris–HCl prior to recording the fluorescence intensities. At pH 7.6, the difference in the fluorescence intensity between the two probe solutions with and without SSZ was the largest (Fig. 3b). Consequently, the Tris–HCl buffer solution (0.05 M) of pH 7.6 was selected for the detection.

Surfactants can significantly influence the formation of complexes and their fluorescence properties [34]. In the experiment, several surfactants, including Tween-80, CTMAB, sodium dodecyl sulfate, sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS), and SDBS, were added to the solutions of the probe in the absence and presence of SSZ, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded. Additionally, the effect of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) was investigated. The results of the evaluation are presented in Figure S4. The comparison of the surfactants and β-CD revealed that the largest quench occurred when SDBS was added.

The optimum concentration of SDBS was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3c, at SDBS concentrations below 1.0 × 10−4 M, an increase of the SDBS concentration resulted in enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of the complex probe in the absence of SSZ, which decreased in the presence of SSZ. Furthermore, when the SDBS concentration was 1.0 × 10−4 M, the difference in the fluorescence intensity between the probe with and without SSZ was the largest.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reaction time of the probe
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the Tb-DPA probe was examined under optimal conditions. As shown in Figure S5, no change in the fluorescence intensity of the probe was observed after 20–120 min, which indicated that the reaction of Tb3+ and DPA was completed within 20 min and the complex was stable for at least 120 min. The probe, therefore, exhibited good stability.

To understand the fluorescent response rate of the probe upon the addition of SSZ, the fluorescent intensity of the probe was measured at different time intervals. As shown in Figure S6, the fluorescence intensity of the probe rapidly decreased and reached stability within 10 min. During the period of 10–80 min, the fluorescence intensity remained nearly unchanged. These results suggested that the response of the probe upon addition of SSZ was fast and stable.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescent response of the probe to different concentrations of SSZ was measured. The fluorescence intensity of the probe at 546 nm gradually decreased with the increase of the SSZ concentration. In addition, good linear relationship between the logarithm of fluorescence intensity of the probe and the concentration of SSZ was observed. The linear regression equations can be expressed as lnI = 6.541–0.1884c (μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9995 in the range of 8.0 × 10−8–1.00 × 10−6 M and lnI = 6.306–0.01546c (μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9965 in the range of 1.00 × 10−6–1.00 × 10−4 M, respectively. The detection limit was calculated to be 60 nM (S/N = 3).

Some of the recently reported methods for the detection of SSZ are summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that the proposed method showed similar or higher sensitivity than the other approaches, with analogous or wider linear ranges.

The reagents for the synthesis of SSZ are aminopyridine and N-acetylamino-benzenesulfonyl chloride. After SSZ is taken by human bodies, the metabolites are sulfadiazine and 5-aminosalicylic acid. Additionally, sulfadiazine and 5-aminosalicylic acid are also two kinds of drug. Since the above substances may coexist with SSZ, it is necessary to discuss whether they may interfere with the determination of SSZ. According to the mechanism for detecting SSZ, it is based on the reaction of adjacent carboxyl groups and hydroxy groups on the benzene ring in SSZ molecule with Tb3+ ions to form a ternary complex (Scheme 1). Considering the molecular structures of aminopyridine, N-acetylamino-benzenesulfonyl chloride and sulfadiazine, it is impossible for them to react with Tb3+ ions to form stable complexes. The molecular structure of 5-aminosalicylic acid is similar to SSZ's, which may interfere with the determination of SSZ, and then the interference experiment was carried out.

Table 2 showed the interference of some inorganic ions and organic substances, which may be present in pharmaceutical preparations and biological fluids, on the detection of SSZ. The tolerance limit was defined as the molar ratio of the maximum concentration of the foreign substance (the concentration that resulted in an error of less than ±5% in the detection of SSZ) to the concentration of SSZ (Table 2). As demonstrated in Table 2, the common substances had little or no effect on the detection of SSZ. Thus, the developed probe was successfully applied for the determination of SSZ with a high selectivity in a complicated matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SSZ in tablets and biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, to examine the feasibility of the method in real samples, the approach was utilized for the detection of SSZ in pharmaceutical tablets, urine, and serum samples (Table 3 ). The content of SSZ in the tablet was determined at 250 mg, which was congruent with the label claim. Furthermore, the recoveries were in the range of 98–105%, with the relative standard derivation (n = 3) of <2.7%. The recoveries of SSZ in the urine samples were 104% to 105%, and 101% to 106% in the serum samples, with the relative standard derivation (n = 3) of <1.5%. The above outcomes demonstrated that our method showed good precision and accuracy in the detection of SSZ in real samples.

In the present study, a new method for the detection of SSZ based on the fluorescence quenching of Tb-DPA complex was established. The mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of Tb-DPA complex by SSZ is static quenching and involved the formation of the SSZ-Tb-DPA ternary complex. In comparison with the previously reported fluorometry approaches, the proposed method is simple, fast, low cost, and does not require any synthesized nanoprobes. Our method has the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, and wide linear range, and can be used for the detection of SSZ in pharmaceutical tablets, urine, and serum samples in routine analysis. Based on the Tb-DPA complex probe, the detection of other non-fluorescent molecules that can be combined with Tb3+ should be investigated in the future.

Jiahui Yan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yongli Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Baoying Kong: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Shuang Zhao: Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51909031).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104553.
